summary: Basecamp replaced its Google Sign-In-based two-factor authentication with a self-built TOTP-based system, eliminating the requirement for users to have a Google account. After initially panicking when SMS-based 2FA was deemed insecure, they had outsourced 2FA to Google as a quick fix but found requiring a Google account to be an uncomfortable privacy compromise. The new system works with any TOTP-compatible app like Authy, 1Password, or Duo. DHH frames this as part of a broader effort to disentangle Basecamp from Big Tech dependencies, including having already dropped Google Analytics.
key_points: SMS-based 2FA was insecure, and Basecamp initially outsourced the problem to Google Sign-In rather than building a proper replacement
Requiring a Google account for 2FA security was an uncomfortable privacy tradeoff given Google's data collection business model
The new TOTP-based system with backup codes works with any compatible authentication app across all Basecamp products
This is part of a deliberate strategy to remove Big Tech dependencies from Basecamp's business, including dropping Google Analytics
money_quotes: There are about a million good reasons for why you wouldn't want Google to know everything about when you log into apps all over the internet.

Google's business is literally based on collecting as much data as possible, so it can use it all against you for ad targeting. That's just not a regime we feel comfortable encouraging, let alone requiring.
It feels good to have one additional area of the business free from Big Tech entanglement.
We'll continue the work to untangle ourselves from Google and the rest of the industry behemoths. It's a long slog, it's unlikely ever to be fully complete, but every little bit helps.
So I'm thrilled to announce that we got our shit together and built our own, wonderful, and secure 2FA login protection for Basecamp.
key_insight: Companies should invest in building their own critical infrastructure rather than outsourcing it to Big Tech, even when the DIY approach seems daunting, because depending on surveillance-capitalism companies creates uncomfortable compromises for your users.
